Observation.goal: Learn to read the prompt exactly, simulate small examples, and state the invariant before reaching for a pattern name. Includes reasoning about what a brute force costs and why.
entry_requirements: No prior stage required; this is the entry point of the curriculum.
Comfortable writing and tracing simple procedural code (loops, conditionals, arrays, strings).
exit_requirements: Restates a problem's input, output, and constraints without losing edge cases.
Builds at least two worked examples before proposing an approach.
Produces a correct brute-force baseline and names its time/space complexity.
Identifies which repeated work in the brute force an invariant could eliminate.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery (see knowledge/skills.json).
Can estimate required complexity directly from stated constraints (e.g. n <= 10^5 implies O(n log n)) without being told.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Jumping to a remembered pattern name before simulating a single example.
Silently dropping a constraint (duplicates, negative numbers, empty input) while restating the problem.
Estimating complexity from habit rather than from the stated input bounds.

State Construction.goal: Build explicit state representations (hash maps, frequency counts, pointer-linked structures) that summarize an input well enough to answer the actual question.
entry_requirements: Observation stage mastered.
Can trace a loop that mutates a running variable and explain what that variable represents at each step.
exit_requirements: Chooses between a hash map, frequency array, or pointer structure based on what needs to be looked up or mutated, not by habit.
Explains what invariant the constructed state preserves as the scan progresses.
Draws pointer state (next/prev links) before writing pointer-mutation code.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can justify why a chosen state representation is sufficient, i.e. why nothing outside it needs to be tracked.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Rebuilding a hash map inside a loop instead of maintaining it incrementally.
Losing a pointer reference before rewiring a linked structure, corrupting the list.
Tracking more state than the problem requires, obscuring the real invariant.

Constraint Maintenance.goal: Maintain a moving invariant (window, pointer pair, or monotonic stack) while scanning, so that repeated work collapses into amortized linear time.
entry_requirements: State Construction stage mastered.
Comfortable with the two-pointer and single-pass scanning idioms from earlier stages.
exit_requirements: Identifies the moving boundary or invariant that controls a window or pointer scan before coding it.
Chooses between pointer movement, window expansion/contraction, and monotonic-stack maintenance without prompting.
Explains, for every push/pop or pointer move, why the discarded element can never be the answer again.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can prove amortized linear time for a monotonic-stack or two-pointer solution rather than asserting it.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Shrinking or growing a window without a clear exit condition, causing off-by-one bugs.
Popping a monotonic stack without checking the invariant that justifies discarding the element.
Reintroducing an O(n^2) rescan instead of trusting the maintained invariant.

Ordered Reasoning.goal: Use sorted order and monotonic feasibility to replace repeated scans with binary search or a single sorted pass.
entry_requirements: Constraint Maintenance stage mastered.
Understands sorting complexity and stability tradeoffs.
exit_requirements: Justifies when sorting first is worth its cost versus scanning unsorted.
Writes a monotonic feasibility check (`can(x)`) before binary-searching an answer space.
Distinguishes searching a sorted array from binary-searching an abstract answer space.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can identify, from a problem statement alone, whether the answer space is monotonic and therefore binary-searchable.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Binary-searching a value space that is not actually monotonic.
Off-by-one errors at the boundary between the last-false and first-true feasibility check.
Choosing a custom comparator that is not a valid total order, producing inconsistent sorts.

Decision Making.goal: Commit to local, irrevocable choices and defend them with an exchange argument, invariant, or counterexample search, instead of exploring every branch.
entry_requirements: Ordered Reasoning stage mastered.
Comfortable sorting or ranking candidates before choosing among them.
exit_requirements: States the greedy rule explicitly before implementing it.
Defends the greedy choice with an exchange argument or a concrete counterexample search, not intuition alone.
Can name a similar-looking problem where the same greedy rule fails, and explain why.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Produces a correctness argument (exchange or matroid-style reasoning) for at least one non-trivial greedy solution without being prompted.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Assuming a greedy choice is correct because it works on the first example.
Applying a memorized greedy rule to a problem whose constraints invalidate it.
Skipping the correctness argument and moving straight to implementation.

Interval Reasoning.goal: Reason about overlapping ranges, sweeps, and ordered range-query structures (Fenwick/segment trees) that summarize or update intervals efficiently.
entry_requirements: Decision Making stage mastered.
Comfortable sorting events or intervals by start/end as a first step.
exit_requirements: Chooses between a sweep-line, interval-merge, or range-structure (Fenwick/segment tree) approach based on whether updates are one-shot or online.
Explains what a segment/Fenwick tree node represents at each level before coding it.
Handles interval-boundary edge cases (touching vs. overlapping) explicitly.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can implement a range-update/range-query structure and justify its complexity without referencing memorized code.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Treating touching intervals as overlapping (or vice versa) without checking the problem's definition.
Rebuilding a range structure from scratch per query instead of supporting incremental updates.
Losing track of what a segment tree node aggregates after a merge.

Recursive Thinking.goal: Decompose hierarchical structures (trees, BSTs, tries) into reusable subproblems and combine subtree results through a stated recursive contract.
entry_requirements: Interval Reasoning stage mastered.
Can trace recursive call stacks by hand for small inputs.
exit_requirements: States the recursive contract (what a call returns and assumes about its subcalls) before traversing.
Separates traversal order (pre/in/post/level) from the business logic applied at each node.
Uses BST ordering or trie prefix structure to prune search work instead of visiting every node.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can convert a recursive tree solution to an iterative one (explicit stack/queue) when asked, without losing correctness.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Mutating shared state across recursive calls without restoring it (missing backtrack/undo).
Conflating traversal order with the aggregation logic, producing tangled recursive code.
Ignoring BST ordering and searching both subtrees when one is provably irrelevant.

State Transition.goal: Define explicit states, transitions, and base cases, then choose an evaluation order that respects dependencies -- the discipline of dynamic programming.
entry_requirements: Recursive Thinking stage mastered.
Can write a correct (if exponential) recursive brute force for an optimization problem.
exit_requirements: Defines state, transition, and base cases in words before writing a recurrence.
Explains why greedy or local reasoning fails on the same problem family, when relevant.
Chooses an evaluation order (top-down memoization vs. bottom-up table) that guarantees dependencies are already solved.
Reduces state dimensionality only after the correct recurrence is established.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can derive a DP recurrence for a novel problem from the recursive brute force, without pattern-matching to a memorized template.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Writing a memo table before the recurrence is proven correct on paper.
Iterating states in an order that reads an unfilled table entry.
Over-compressing state before verifying the full-dimensional version is correct.

Graph Thinking.goal: Model problems as graphs, choose the right traversal or connectivity tool, and separate reachability/traversal correctness from path optimality.
entry_requirements: State Transition stage mastered.
Comfortable with queue- and stack-based traversal mechanics from Constraint Maintenance and State Construction.
exit_requirements: Defines nodes, edges, and visited state explicitly before choosing an algorithm.
Chooses between BFS, DFS, Union-Find, topological sort, and shortest-path algorithms with a stated reason.
Explains the difference between 'a path exists' (traversal) and 'this is the best path' (optimization).
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can pick the correct shortest-path algorithm (BFS/Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford) based on edge-weight properties stated in the problem.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Revisiting nodes without correct visited-state tracking, causing infinite loops or wrong counts.
Using DFS/BFS reachability where an actual shortest-path algorithm is required.
Forgetting to detect negative cycles or disconnected components when the problem allows them.

Query Processing.goal: Answer repeated queries efficiently using precomputed prefix structures or order-statistic heaps, instead of rescanning the input per query.
entry_requirements: Graph Thinking stage mastered.
Comfortable with prefix-sum arithmetic and heap invariants from earlier stages.
exit_requirements: Uses prefix sums or prefix-hash combinations to convert repeated range queries into O(1) or amortized-constant checks.
Chooses between a single heap, two heaps, or top-k heap based on what statistic the query needs.
States what invariant a heap maintains (e.g. 'the smaller half is a max-heap') before coding it.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can design an online query-answering structure (heap- or prefix-based) for a novel streaming problem.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Recomputing a prefix sum per query instead of precomputing once.
Letting two heaps drift out of balance, breaking the median/order-statistic invariant.
Using a single sort where an online, incrementally-updated structure is required.

Pattern Discovery.goal: Generate the space of possibilities deliberately, prune aggressively using the problem's constraints, and recognize when a decision tree is the right model.
entry_requirements: Query Processing stage mastered.
Comfortable writing and tracing recursive backtracking code with explicit undo steps.
exit_requirements: Defines the decision tree (choices at each node, and what closes a branch) before recursing.
Prunes branches using problem constraints rather than generating everything and filtering afterward.
Distinguishes combination, permutation, and constraint-satisfaction backtracking by what they hold fixed (order, uniqueness, or feasibility).
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can add memoization to a backtracking solution when subproblems repeat, and explain what makes a state cacheable.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Forgetting to undo a choice (state, visited-set) before backtracking, corrupting later branches.
Generating duplicate combinations/permutations due to a missing 'skip equal siblings' check.
Pruning too late -- after full recursion into a branch that constraints already ruled out.

Mathematical Thinking.goal: Replace simulation with direct reasoning using number-theoretic or bitwise properties, and prove correctness algebraically rather than by example.
entry_requirements: Pattern Discovery stage mastered.
Comfortable with modular arithmetic and binary representation basics.
exit_requirements: States the number-theoretic or bit-level property being exploited before writing code.
Distinguishes problems that need exact arithmetic reasoning from ones that only look numeric on the surface.
Verifies an arithmetic shortcut against brute force on small cases before trusting it.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can prove (not just test) a closed-form or bitwise identity used in a solution.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Integer overflow or sign-bit mistakes when reasoning about bit manipulation.
Asserting a numeric pattern from a few examples without proving it holds generally.
Reaching for simulation on a problem whose constraints demand a closed-form or O(log n) approach.

Integration.goal: Compose multiple data structures into a durable system, support online updates, and reason about API guarantees under sustained use -- the terminal stage of the apprenticeship.
entry_requirements: Mathematical Thinking stage mastered, and at least one skill mastered in each of the other 12 stages.
Comfortable designing a class-level API (not just a single function) with documented method contracts.
exit_requirements: Chooses storage, indexing, and update rules as part of the design, not as an afterthought bolted onto a working prototype.
Keeps interface guarantees (e.g. O(1) amortized) aligned with the actual operational complexity under sustained use.
Explains the tradeoff between a simpler implementation and one with stronger asymptotic guarantees, and picks deliberately.
Completes the Implementation Blueprint before coding in this stage: state, initialization, loop bounds, previous-state needs, answer maintenance, and return value.
mastery_criteria: All skills in this stage reach skill mastery.
Can design a system (e.g. an LRU cache, rate limiter, or streaming median service) end-to-end and defend every data-structure choice against an alternative.
Demonstrates Implementation Engineering for this stage by deriving initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, and return value from the state definition rather than memorized code.
common_failure_modes: Optimizing one operation (e.g. insert) while silently degrading another (e.g. lookup) below its stated guarantee.
Skipping capacity/eviction edge cases that only appear under sustained use, not in a single test call.
Combining data structures without re-verifying that their combined invariants still hold.
